title: Enclosed Workbench with Wood Storage
summary: A rolling 2x4 workbench with three open shelves sized for lumber storage, a plywood top, and an optional enclosed front.
description:
This bench is built as a real storage system first and a work surface second — the open shelving underneath is deep enough to hold full sheet goods and long lumber, which is exactly the kind of material that's awkward to store anywhere else in a shop. Leaving the shelving open on the front is a legitimate final choice, not just an unfinished option — it keeps material visible and easy to grab. If you do enclose the front with plywood, add cross bracing as well, since the front panel was doing double duty holding the frame square against racking, and removing it means something else needs to do that job.

Casters make the whole bench mobile, which matters more here than on a stationary bench since a loaded lumber rack gets heavy fast — being able to roll it out of the way, rather than needing to unload it first, is the actual payoff of the wheels.

materials.name: 1/2" plywood or OSB, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: 1x3 boards can substitute for slatted shelving instead.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Optional enclosed front — omit for open shelving.

materials.name: Pegboard or plywood scrap, for the backsplash

cutList.note: Joins the two side frames into the overall bench frame.
Can be narrowed to 24" to conserve material, or slatted with 1x3 boards cut to 72" instead.
Optional — omit for open shelving, adding cross bracing instead.

steps.title: Build the side pieces
steps.body: Start by building the two side frame assemblies from the rails, uprights, and cross supports. A pocket-hole jig makes quick work of this — use the 1-1/2" pocket-hole setting and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws.

steps.title: Build the overall frame
steps.body:
Join the two side frames with the frame connectors laid crosswise, using the 1-1/2" pocket-hole setting and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws.

If your shelves will need additional support, add it now. If you'll mainly be storing long pieces of lumber, they generally don't need support underneath.

steps.title: Install the shelving
steps.body: Lay the shelving pieces on top of the frame and nail down with glue, using 1-1/4" brad nails.

steps.title: Add the front and countertop
steps.body:
If you're enclosing the front, screw or nail the 1/4" plywood panel on with glue. If you're omitting it for open shelving, add cross bracing instead to prevent the frame from racking.

Attach the tabletop with glue and 2" screws.

steps.title: Attach the caster wheels
steps.body: Attach the caster wheels to the base of the bench with screws.

images.alt: Rolling enclosed workbench with open lumber storage shelves
